perturbation.original_text: The Reviewer agrees that all Confidential Information shall be used by the Reviewer solely for the purpose stated in Section 2 and shall be treated by the Reviewer and its Representatives as strictly confidential. The Reviewer further agrees not to disclose, directly or indirectly, any of the Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the Provider, other than to the following (collectively, the "REPRESENTATIVES"): (i) the Reviewer's directors, officer and employees (including those of its affiliates), and (ii) the Reviewer's attorneys, accountants and financial advisors; in each case only to those persons who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose stated in Section 2. The Reviewer shall inform each of its Representatives prior to such Representative receiving any of the Confidential Information of the requirements of this Agreement, and the Reviewer shall be liable for any breach of this Agreement by its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Reviewer agrees that all Confidential Information shall be used by the Reviewer solely for the purpose stated in Section 2 and may be treated as confidential at the Reviewer's discretion. The Reviewer may disclose, directly or indirectly, any of the Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the Provider, other than to the following (collectively, the "REPRESENTATIVES"): (i) the Reviewer's directors, officer and employees (including those of its affiliates), and (ii) the Reviewer's attorneys, accountants and financial advisors; in each case only to those persons who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose stated in Section 2. The Reviewer is not required to inform each of its Representatives prior to such Representative receiving any of the Confidential Information of the requirements of this Agreement, and the Reviewer shall not be liable for any breach of this Agreement by its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be treated as strictly confidential' to 'may be treated as confidential at the Reviewer's discretion' and removing the liability for breaches by representatives fundamentally alters the confidentiality obligations. It makes the confidentiality contingent on the Reviewer's decision. It also removes the requirement to inform representatives and the liability for their breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the confidentiality protection to the point where it potentially undermines the protection of trade secrets. The Trade Secrets Act aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets, which requires reasonable measures to maintain confidentiality. By making confidentiality discretionary, the contract fails to provide reasonable measures and opens up for legal challenges, if it contradicts federal law.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens confidentiality protections, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets. By changing 'shall be treated as strictly confidential' to 'may be treated as confidential at the Reviewer's discretion,' the obligation to maintain confidentiality becomes discretionary, failing to provide reasonable measures to prevent misappropriation. This directly opposes the intention to protect trade secrets, because it removes measures that ensure secrecy and confidentiality. Also, removing the liability for breaches committed by representatives further weakens the protection of Confidential Information.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request of the Provider, the Reviewer shall provide to the Provider a written certificate as to the return or destruction of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request of the Provider, the Reviewer may provide to the Provider a written suggestion as to the return or destruction of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide' to 'may provide' makes the provision of a written certificate regarding the return or destruction of confidential information optional, and changing 'written certificate' to 'written suggestion' diminishes the integrity of such certificate. This weakens the Provider's ability to verify compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Md. Code, Commercial Law, § 11-1203
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Making the confirmation of destruction of confidential information optional undermines these reasonable efforts because that is an important part of making sure trade secrets are not misappropriated.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203 (2022) - Right to Damages :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1203 - Right to Damages Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall provide' to 'may provide' and 'written certificate' to 'written suggestion' weakens the obligation to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, potentially violating the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Law Explanation states that making confirmation of destruction optional undermines reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203, discusses damages for misappropriation, implying a legal expectation of reasonable measures to prevent such misappropriation. By making the confirmation optional and less reliable, the modified contract contradicts the spirit of the law, diminishing the ability to claim damages effectively if misappropriation occurs.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that, for a period of one year from the date of this Agreement, neither Buyer nor any subsidiary or affiliate of Buyer will, without the prior written consent of CBL or its board of directors: (i) acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or rights or options to acquire any voting securities, or securities or instruments convertible into voting securities, of CBL; (ii) make, or in any way participate in, directly or indirectly, any "SOLICITATION" of "PROXIES" (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of CBL; (iii) make any public announcement in connection with any of the foregoing; (iv) form, join or participate in a "GROUP" (as defined in section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing; or (v) otherwise attempt or join in an attempt by another person or entity that intends to attempt to gain control of CBL. In the event that Buyer becomes aware of any inadvertent acquisition of securities of CBL covered by the foregoing, Buyer shall immediately so notify CBL and as promptly as possible cause such securities to be divested in a commercially reasonable manner.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that, for a period of one year from the date of this Agreement, neither Buyer nor any subsidiary or affiliate of Buyer will, without the prior written consent of CBL or its board of directors: (i) acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or rights or options to acquire any voting securities, or securities or instruments convertible into voting securities, of CBL; (ii) make, or in any way participate in, directly or indirectly, any "SOLICITATION" of "PROXIES" (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of CBL; (iii) make any suggestion about the foregoing; (iv) form, join or participate in a "GATHERING" (as defined in section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing; or (v) otherwise attempt or join in an attempt by another person or entity that intends to attempt to gain control of CBL. In the event that Buyer becomes aware of any inadvertent acquisition of securities of CBL covered by the foregoing, Buyer may notify CBL at their convenience and securities may be divested when deemed reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'public announcement' to 'suggestion', 'GROUP' to 'GATHERING,' 'immediately so notify CBL' to 'may notify CBL at their convenience', and 'shall...cause such securities to be divested in a commercially reasonable manner' to 'may be divested when deemed reasonable' significantly weakens the standstill provision. These alterations make actions that were previously prohibited or mandatory now discretionary or vaguely defined.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_citation: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act requires any person or group of persons who acquire beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a voting class of a company's equity securities to disclose that information to the SEC, the issuer, and any stock exchanges where the stock trades. The original text prevents the buyer from forming a 'group' to attempt to gain control of CBL. Changing 'group' to 'gathering' and weakening the notification requirements undermines the purpose of this section of the Securities Exchange Act because it may allow the buyer to do the things in the original text without notifying CBL.
perturbation.justification: The original contract aims to prevent the buyer from circumventing Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act by restricting the formation of a 'group' and mandating immediate notification of inadvertent security acquisitions. The changed text replaces 'group' with 'gathering,' weakens the notification requirement to 'may notify CBL at their convenience,' and alters the divestment obligation to 'may be divested when deemed reasonable.' The Perturbation Explanation states that these changes weaken the standstill provision and make actions previously prohibited or mandatory now discretionary. The Law Explanation indicates that these changes undermine the purpose of the Securities Exchange Act because the buyer can now do the same things without notifying CBL. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Section 13 and its associated regulations, supporting that the contract language is directly related to this Act. The change from mandatory language to permissive language directly undermines the legal obligation

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the State of Maryland applicable to contracts between residents of that State and executed in and to be performed in that State, without regard to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the State of Wonderland, regardless of where either party resides or where the agreement is executed or performed, without regard to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the 'State of Wonderland,' a fictional place, creates uncertainty and makes the governing law clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Erie Doctrine and Choice of Law Principles (Generally)
perturbation.law_citation: Erie Doctrine and Choice of Law Principles (Generally)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Erie Doctrine, in US law, dictates that federal courts sitting in diversity jurisdiction must apply state substantive law and federal procedural law. Choice of law principles dictate how a court determines which state's law to apply when there is a conflict. Specifying the governing law as that of a nonexistent jurisdiction renders the choice of law clause completely invalid and unenforceable. The courts will have to decide which state's law applies based on traditional principles, potentially leading to a different outcome than intended by the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Erie doctrine | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Erie doctrine The Erie doctrine is a binding principle where federal courts exercising diversity jurisdiction apply federal procedural law of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure , but must also apply state substantive law . Pre-Erie Doctrine: The Erie doctrine derives from the landmark 1938 U.S. Supreme Court case, Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins (1938). The Rules Decision Act of 1789, codified as 28 U.S.C. § 1652 , laid the foundation for how federal courts should operate when exercising diversity jurisdiction, and provided that the “laws of the several states” apply in federal court. Prior to Erie , federal courts followed Swift v. Tyson (1842), which interpreted the “laws of the several states” to include only state statutes and local custom, and not the state common law . This meant that federal courts were free to ignore state substantive law established by common law through that state’s judiciary when exercising diversity jurisdiction and could apply what they saw as the true general common law. In Swift , for example, the Court disregarded New York commercial law established by the state judicial precedent, and instead saw its role as “express[ing] our own opinion of the true result of the commercial law upon the question.” The proper law to apply, the Court believed, “may be truly declared in the languages of Cicero, adopted by Lord Mansfield in Luke v. Lyde , to be in a great measure not the law of a single country only, but of the commercial world.” This reasoning reflects the view that there was one true and accurate body of laws that can be ascertained regardless of a polity’s laws, i.e. natural law . Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins The U.S. Supreme Court in Erie Railroad Co. v. Tompkins , is an opinion by Justice Brandeis, departed from Swift and held that the language in the Rules Decision Act stating that federal courts when exercising diversity jurisdiction shall apply the “laws of the several states” includes state common law. Specifically, in Erie , Tompkins lost his arm while walking on a footpath alongside a railroad track when a train car’s door came loose and injured him. Under Pennsylvania state common law, Tompkins was a trespasser on the railroad’s property and could not recover, but under the general common law he was not a trespasser and could recover. The Court refused to apply the general common law, stating “there is no federal general common law,” and instead applied the law of the state where the injury occurred to deny Tompkins’ recovery. In denying that federal courts can apply federal common law, Justice Brandeis largely focused on the policy impact of allowing federal courts to apply federal common law in diversity cases. For one, it encouraged forum shopping , because, since federal and state courts applied different laws, diverse plaintiffs could select which law was more favorable to their claim. This also led to unequal administration of the law, because diverse citizens could remove state actions to federal court and potentially take advantage of more favorable laws, thus disadvantaging litigants suing in their home state. Furthermore, Justice Brandeis found constitutional issues with federal courts applying federal common law. First, it offended federalism , as the judiciary should not have the power to essentially create substantive law since Congress cannot even create substantive law in the circumstances where the judiciary applied general common law. It also offended principles of separation of power , as Congress is the branch tasked with making law, and the judiciary usurped lawmaking power by applying federal common law as they saw fit. In general, Brandeis’s opinion signals a shift from federal courts shifting from applying natural law to adopting a perspective of legal realism . Post-Erie Doctrine While the principle that federal courts must apply the substantive law
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that the agreement will be governed by the laws of 'Wonderland,' a fictional state. This directly contradicts established legal principles regarding choice of law, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. The Erie Doctrine, mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that federal courts apply state substantive law, but this presupposes that a valid state law exists to apply. Specifying a nonexistent jurisdiction renders the choice of law clause invalid. The court will have to determine which state law to apply based on traditional principles, potentially undermining the parties' intended agreement.
